name: curl
context: HTTP client for making API requests, transferring data, testing endpoints, and debugging network issues from the command line
info: The curl ecosystem covers the command-line HTTP client that agents use for all direct HTTP interactions: calling REST and GraphQL APIs, uploading and downloading files, testing endpoints, inspecting response headers, and diagnosing connectivity problems. curl is available on virtually every system and supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, HTTPS, FTP, SFTP, and dozens of other protocols. Agents reach for curl when they need to make a quick API call without writing a script, when they need to debug request/response cycles with verbose output, or when they need to automate file transfers. The tree provides focused coverage: [[curl/request]] for constructing and executing HTTP requests, [[curl/auth]] for authentication patterns (Basic, Bearer, OAuth, mTLS), and [[curl/debugging]] for verbose output, tracing, timing, and diagnosing failures. curl is the HTTP substrate — most API ecosystems ([[github]], [[anthropic]], [[linear]], [[supabase]]) can be driven directly through curl before graduating to SDK-based approaches.
triggers: download a file from a url
check if a url is reachable
body:
# curl Ecosystem

curl is the universal command-line tool for transferring data with URLs. It speaks HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SFTP, SCP, and dozens of other protocols. For agents, curl is the go-to tool for making API calls, testing endpoints, downloading files, and diagnosing HTTP issues without writing application code.

## Tree Structure

| Node | Type | Purpose |
|------|------|---------|
| [[curl/request]] | skill | Construct and execute HTTP requests — GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, headers, body, query params |
| [[curl/auth]] | context | Authentication patterns — Basic, Bearer tokens, OAuth, API keys, client certificates |
| [[curl/debugging]] | context | Verbose output, response timing, tracing, diagnosing failures, write-out format strings |

## Why curl?

curl is installed on every Linux, macOS, and modern Windows system. It requires no runtime, no package manager, no SDK installation. When an agent needs to:

- **Test an API** before writing integration code → `curl -s https://api.example.com/health`
- **Send a webhook payload** → `curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"event":"deploy"}' https://hooks.example.com`
- **Download a file** → `curl -LO https://releases.example.com/v1.0/binary`
- **Check connectivity** → `curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://api.example.com`

curl handles it with a single command.

## Quick Reference

```bash
# Simple GET
curl https://api.example.com/users

# GET with headers
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
     -H "Accept: application/json" \
     https://api.example.com/users

# POST JSON
curl -X POST \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"name": "Alice", "email": "alice@example.com"}' \
     https://api.example.com/users

# PUT update
curl -X PUT \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"name": "Alice Updated"}' \
     https://api.example.com/users/42

# DELETE
curl -X DELETE https://api.example.com/users/42

# Download file with progress
curl -LO https://releases.example.com/v2.0/app.tar.gz

# Upload file
curl -X POST -F "file=@report.pdf" https://api.example.com/upload

# Silent with status code only
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://api.example.com/health
```

## Exit Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 0 | Success |
| 6 | Could not resolve host |
| 7 | Failed to connect to host |
| 22 | HTTP error (with `--fail`) |
| 28 | Operation timeout |
| 35 | SSL connect error |
| 51 | SSL peer certificate problem |
| 52 | Empty reply from server |
| 56 | Failure receiving data |

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `http_proxy` / `HTTP_PROXY` | HTTP proxy URL |
| `https_proxy` / `HTTPS_PROXY` | HTTPS proxy URL |
| `no_proxy` / `NO_PROXY` | Comma-separated list of hosts to bypass proxy |
| `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` | Path to CA certificate bundle |
| `HOME/.curlrc` | Default config file read on every invocation |

sections.title: HTTP methods
sections.body: curl defaults to GET. Use `-X POST`, `-X PUT`, `-X DELETE`, `-X PATCH`, or `-X HEAD` to override. The `-I` flag is shorthand for HEAD. When you use `-d` (data), curl automatically switches to POST. The `-G` flag converts `-d` data into query string parameters on a GET request. For most API work: GET to read, POST to create, PUT/PATCH to update, DELETE to remove.

sections.title: Common agent patterns
sections.body:
**Health check**: `curl -sf https://api.example.com/health` — silent, fail on error, exit code tells the story.
**JSON API call**: `curl -s -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' https://api.example.com/resource | jq .` — pipe to jq for parsing.
**File download**: `curl -LO https://url/file` — follow redirects, use remote filename.
**Status code only**: `curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://url` — discard body, print status.
**Retry on failure**: `curl --retry 3 --retry-delay 2 https://api.example.com/resource` — automatic retry with backoff.
**Parallel requests**: `curl -Z https://api1.example.com https://api2.example.com` — HTTP/2 multiplexing.

sections.title: curl vs alternatives
sections.body: **curl vs wget**: wget is better for recursive site downloads; curl is better for API work, supports more protocols, and offers finer control over request construction. **curl vs httpie**: httpie has nicer default output and JSON syntax, but curl is universally installed and scripts depend on it. **curl vs fetch/axios**: SDK HTTP clients offer programmatic control, type safety, and middleware pipelines, but curl is zero-dependency and ideal for quick tests, CI scripts, and agent exploration. Use curl for ad-hoc requests and debugging; graduate to SDK clients for production application code.

links.context: Construct and execute HTTP requests with all methods, headers, and body types
Authentication patterns for APIs — Bearer, Basic, API keys, mTLS
Verbose output, timing, tracing, and diagnosing HTTP failures
curl runs in a Bash shell — see Bash for execution patterns
Pipe curl output to jq, grep, or other processors
Environment variables for proxy, CA bundles, and config

related.note: curl is invoked through the shell — Bash execution context is required
curl output is typically piped to jq, grep, or wc for processing
GitHub APIs can be called directly with curl before using the gh CLI or Octokit
Anthropic Messages API can be called directly with curl
Supabase REST APIs are testable with curl

author: dojo-community
